item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A property that essentially boils down to being anomalously predictable really doesn't make for that interesting of a story. Yes, the tropes are consciously embraced, but being aware of the tropey nature of something doesn't fix the inherent issues that it's been done before several times.

item.title: Admin Post - Info - Open
item.content:encoded:
Noting that a recent edit by Qwags was reverted, as it incorrectly introduced a url relating to a page in the "deleted:" category.

Such pages are considered improperly deleted, and per the Deletion Guidelines, are routinely removed by staff and considered summary deletions. Editors, please refrain from linking to any pages with "deleted:" in the title.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
In my totally unbiased opinion, I support S & C Plastics being included in this, though I might word the footnote a bit differently; will edit to add a suggestion.

(Also how did I not see this skip before? Dang it, Bryx, ya gotta tell me when ya do stuff like this!)

ETA:

In this case, causality within SCP-2920 is altered to conform to common human superstitions involving certain events, seasons, and activities.1

Footnotes
1. Investigation into thematic similarities between anomalies manifesting in Nx-59 and Nx-18 have been noted. Investigation into any connections are ongoing.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Time Expired. Edits made as discussed below. Author may revert at any time.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team

+ Show Block
- Hide Block

Two potential crosslinks here. The first, is an inline link to SCP-1000-J, which Bryx has gone on record as stating that this article and the Old-Wives Town are basically one and the same. The second, the nexus featured in this article has a lot of similarities to the nexus featured in S and C Plastics, so a brief mention of the similarities wouldn't be too far out of line. As such, I am recommending the following edits:

Changing this:

In this case, causality within SCP-2920 is altered to conform to common human superstitions involving certain events, seasons, and activities.

To this:

In this case, causality within SCP-2920 is altered to conform to common human superstitions involving certain events, seasons, and activities.1

If anyone has other/better examples that could be used here please leave a comment saying so!

Footnotes
1. Similarity between the effects of Nx-59 and the Nx-18 has been noted, with investigation into the shared effects ongoing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Good stuff Bryx. So badly want to find an argument to make this Safe, but it's probably me just feeling for the citizens.

Hadn't read 1000-J before now but I'll give it a look.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hey, cool, all my stuff is still here.

Did anyone ever figure out that this is just SCP-1000-J?

item.title: Re: SCP-2920
item.content:encoded: More like Night Vale under Strex control. Unfortunately, 2920 does not have a small army of anomalous beings, and angels, to help with liberation efforts.

item.title: SCP-2920
item.content:encoded: Sounds sorta like NightVale, except not. Nice SCP. 1+

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
People vs Authority isn't a purely American thing; same thing happens everywhere: take the Bristol Riots and Peterloo Massacre in England, the Troubles in Ireland, the Bloody Revolution in France… we're not the only ones who started their modern government with a series of angry riots.

What's going on here is that this place takes stereotypes, urban legends, superstitions, tropes, etc. and makes them real. In this case, what it's making real is the situation you see in movies like 28 Weeks Later1- a faceless authority quarantines an area for the good of everyone; the people inside of said area then unwittingly doom the world by heroically rising up against their oppressors… and the Foundation, meanwhile, is having trouble resisting 2920's attempts to apply the Tautological Templar and Villain Ball tropes to their containment of SCP-2920.

Footnotes
1. Which you'll note is set in the UK

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Late to the party as usual.

I'm a little surprised that no one compared the story playing out here to the American Revolution. From my foreigner's perspective this seems like a distinct echo of some of the events I've heard of, especially the massacre in the town square.

Maybe my American cousins consider it too obvious to mention or know exactly where it doesn't fit.

I'm novoting for now as it did interest me but it didn't quite get to me enough.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm going to echo others here. The clinical tone and style are solid, so that's not the issue.

The anomaly itself isn't very interesting. The Nexus traits are serviceable, but the "and it can escape!" thread is… very basic. The suggestion that it is an independent anomaly is interesting, but not explored.

As Kalinin pointed out, the play-out is predictable. This could almost be an NBC show.

(Plus, pure personal bias here, but I tend to hold Nexus stories to a higher standard, especially when we're talking about raw human behavior. That's the whole point of S&C Plastics, even though that leans strongly towards comedy. I don't think this quite lives up.)

If you intended to make the implication that the Foundation was behaving as it is because of the Nexus's properties, falling into the role of the MIB or FEMA-takeover-conspiracy government, that should have been made clearer or worked more extensively into the narrative.

As it stands this does nothing for me. -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Unfortunately, the "this is how it's expected to go, and therefore it does" conceit of the article works against it. The narrative, indeed, goes about how I expected it to go. As a result, the story feels like one I've encountered before, and the horror of the real-world versions of it make this telling pale in comparison. On the whole, it's not doing a whole lot for me.

item.title: Talk about your vicious cycle . . .
item.content:encoded:
Fascinating yet mournful to see the Foundation caught in the SCP field itself, yet unable to break out of what "everyone knows" shadowy organizations like their do to people.

+1… and a few sad sighs.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I asked myself, does the Foundation not realize where this is heading? They are creating a situation where the oppressed stand up and break out against their captors, the underdog story we've seen so many times.

Then I realized, the Foundation can't stop it. The SCP's anomalous effect itself is what ensures the events happen in, for lack of a better term, narratively expected ways. You'd expect a fictional shady organisation to be untrustworthy and unreasonably evil. Therefore events transpire to make the Foundation be just that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoted hard. I'm a fan of Outbreak, so I'm a fan of this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is a good treatise, an SCP with a message. I thought the town's anomalous properties were fairly interesting, and have to wonder if they don't play a part in the degradation of relations between the town and the Foundation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I remember wondering to myself what would happen if the Foundation had to openly (to the residents) contain a city or town. This certainly satisfies that curiosity. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yeah…nicely played out, with the incident reports following the updates in containment. The anomalous properties aren't that exciting, but the police state made for some excellent tension.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Love the "think about it" layer to this one's arc. Not really even sure where they could begin to undo the vicious cycle here, but at least the effect is helping the containment. At least till they start getting ideas about the underdog always winning.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: JFC, Bryx. This is pretty fucking dark. Congrats?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks to psul for forum critique.

This author commentary has been brought to you by Spoiler-O's
May aggravate dairy allergies

What do you really expect to happen when a blameless small town is put under quarantine by a vast, shadowy organization for the greater good? Reasonable, fair-minded cooperation between the town and the organization to achieve the most universally agreeable situation possible, or iron-fisted dictatorship, police on every street corner, corruption, and widespread subjugation? Whatever you expect to happen does in this SCP.
